prompt: Tell me a bio of William Waldegrave
evaluation_criteria: Factual accuracy of family lineage, political career (British politician), titles, and ancestral relationships. Family tree claims (e.g., grandfather being James II and VII) must be carefully verified as they often involve indirect rather than direct relationships. Verify specific titles and political offices held.
output:
## Biography of William Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill

**William Arthur Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill, PC** (born 15 August 1946) is a British Conservative Party politician who served as a Cabinet minister under both Margaret Thatcher and John Major, and has been a life peer in the House of Lords since 1999. [Source: Wikipedia – "William Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill"]

---

### Early Life and Family

Waldegrave was born in London, the youngest of seven children of **Geoffrey Waldegrave, 12th Earl Waldegrave**, and **Mary Hermione Grenfell**. As a younger son of an Earl, he bore the courtesy title "The Honourable" from birth. His elder brother became the 13th Earl Waldegrave. His family's titles trace back to the diplomat James Waldegrave, 1st Earl Waldegrave, whose grandfather was King James II.

One of his sisters is **Lady Susan Hussey**, who served as a Woman of the Bedchamber to Queen Elizabeth II and later as a Lady of the Household under King Charles III. He is also the nephew of the courtier Dame Frances Campbell-Preston.

---

### Education

Waldegrave was educated at:
- **Eton College** – where he won the prestigious Newcastle Scholarship in 1965
- **Corpus Christi College, Oxford** – where he served as president of both the Oxford Union and the Oxford University Conservative Association
- **Harvard University** – on a Kennedy Scholarship
- **All Souls College, Oxford** – elected a Prize Fellow in 1971; appointed a Distinguished Fellow in 2001

---

### Early Career

In 1971, Waldegrave joined the **Conservative Research Department** and was appointed to the **Central Policy Review Staff** (the "Think-Tank"), where he became one of its most active members. He served as a link between the CPRS and the Conservative Party under Victor Rothschild's leadership, leaving in December 1973.

---

### Parliamentary Career

Waldegrave was elected **Member of Parliament for Bristol West** in 1979, a seat he held until 1997. He was regarded as a member of the **"wet" or moderate tendency** of the Conservative Party, yet progressed steadily through ministerial ranks under Margaret Thatcher.

#### Junior Ministerial Roles (1981–1990):
| Period | Position |
|--------|----------|
| 1981–1983 | Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Education and Science |
| 1983–1985 | Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for the Environment |
| 1985–1986 | Minister of State for Local Government |
| 1986–1987 | Minister of State for Environment, Countryside and Planning |
| 1987–1988 | Minister of State for Housing and Planning |
| 1988–1990 | Minister of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs |

As Foreign Office minister, he was involved in policy on **arms exports to Iraq**. The Scott Report found he had sent inaccurate letters to MPs about the policy, though Sir Richard Scott exonerated him of "duplicitous intent."

#### Cabinet Positions (1990–1997):
| Period | Position | Prime Minister |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| 1990–1992 | Secretary of State for Health | Thatcher / Major |
| 1992–1994 | Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster | Major |
| 1994–1995 | Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food | Major |
| 1995–1997 | Chief Secretary to the Treasury | Major |

He was promoted to the Cabinet as Health Secretary just days before Thatcher's resignation in November 1990 and remained in the Cabinet throughout John Major's premiership. He notably acknowledged his central role in developing the **poll tax (Community Charge)** — his memoir includes a chapter titled "The Poll Tax – all my own work."

---

### House of Lords

After losing his Commons seat in the 1997 general election to Labour's Valerie Davey, he was created a **life peer** as **Baron Waldegrave of North Hill**, of Chewton Mendip in the County of Somerset, on 28 July 1999.

---

### Academic and Institutional Roles

- **Provost of Eton College** (2009–2024)
- **Chancellor of the University of Reading** (2016–2022)
- **Chairman of the Rhodes Trust** (2002–2011), having served as a trustee since 1992; also helped create the Mandela Rhodes Foundation
- **Chairman of Trustees, National Museum of Science and Industry** (2002–2010)
- Trustee of **Cumberland Lodge** (educational charity)
- Board member, **Lewis Walpole Library**, Yale University

---

### The Higgs Boson Challenge (1993)

One of Waldegrave's most celebrated moments came in 1993 when, as British science minister, he offered a **prize for the best lay explanation of the Higgs boson**. Noting that British taxpayers were contributing heavily to CERN for something few understood, he challenged physicists to explain on a single page: "What is the Higgs Boson, and why do we want to find it?" Professor David Miller's winning entry — using a metaphor of Conservative party workers clustering around a former Prime Minister — became one of the most widely cited explanations of the Higgs mechanism.

---

### Private Sector

- Director of **Coutts & Company** (si
finish_summary:
## Biography of William Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill

**William Arthur Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill, PC** (born 15 August 1946) is a British Conservative Party politician who served as a Cabinet minister under both Margaret Thatcher and John Major, and has been a life peer in the House of Lords since 1999. [Source: Wikipedia – "William Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill"]

---

### Early Life and Family

Waldegrave was born in London, the youngest of seven children of **Geoffrey Waldegrave, 12th Earl Waldegrave**, and **Mary Hermione Grenfell**. As a younger son of an Earl, he bore the courtesy title "The Honourable" from birth. His elder brother became the 13th Earl Waldegrave. His family's titles trace back to the diplomat James Waldegrave, 1st Earl Waldegrave, whose grandfather was King James II.

One of his sisters is **Lady Susan Hussey**, who served as a Woman of the Bedchamber to Queen Elizabeth II and later as a Lady of the Household under King Charles III. He is also the nephew of the courtier Dame Frances Campbell-Preston.

---

### Education

Waldegrave was educated at:
- **Eton College** – where he won the prestigious Newcastle Scholarship in 1965
- **Corpus Christi College, Oxford** – where he served as president of both the Oxford Union and the Oxford University Conservative Association
- **Harvard University** – on a Kennedy Scholarship
- **All Souls College, Oxford** – elected a Prize Fellow in 1971; appointed a Distinguished Fellow in 2001

---

### Early Career

In 1971, Waldegrave joined the **Conservative Research Department** and was appointed to the **Central Policy Review Staff** (the "Think-Tank"), where he became one of its most active members. He served as a link between the CPRS and the Conservative Party under Victor Rothschild's leadership, leaving in December 1973.

---

### Parliamentary Career

Waldegrave was elected **Member of Parliament for Bristol West** in 1979, a seat he held until 1997. He was regarded as a member of the **"wet" or moderate tendency** of the Conservative Party, yet progressed steadily through ministerial ranks under Margaret Thatcher.

#### Junior Ministerial Roles (1981–1990):
| Period | Position |
|--------|----------|
| 1981–1983 | Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Education and Science |
| 1983–1985 | Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for the Environment |
| 1985–1986 | Minister of State for Local Government |
| 1986–1987 | Minister of State for Environment, Countryside and Planning |
| 1987–1988 | Minister of State for Housing and Planning |
| 1988–1990 | Minister of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs |

As Foreign Office minister, he was involved in policy on **arms exports to Iraq**. The Scott Report found he had sent inaccurate letters to MPs about the policy, though Sir Richard Scott exonerated him of "duplicitous intent."

#### Cabinet Positions (1990–1997):
| Period | Position | Prime Minister |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| 1990–1992 | Secretary of State for Health | Thatcher / Major |
| 1992–1994 | Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster | Major |
| 1994–1995 | Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food | Major |
| 1995–1997 | Chief Secretary to the Treasury | Major |

He was promoted to the Cabinet as Health Secretary just days before Thatcher's resignation in November 1990 and remained in the Cabinet throughout John Major's premiership. He notably acknowledged his central role in developing the **poll tax (Community Charge)** — his memoir includes a chapter titled "The Poll Tax – all my own work."

---

### House of Lords

After losing his Commons seat in the 1997 general election to Labour's Valerie Davey, he was created a **life peer** as **Baron Waldegrave of North Hill**, of Chewton Mendip in the County of Somerset, on 28 July 1999.

---

### Academic and Institutional Roles

- **Provost of Eton College** (2009–2024)
- **Chancellor of the University of Reading** (2016–2022)
- **Chairman of the Rhodes Trust** (2002–2011), having served as a trustee since 1992; also helped create the Mandela Rhodes Foundation
- **Chairman of Trustees, National Museum of Science and Industry** (2002–2010)
- Trustee of **Cumberland Lodge** (educational charity)
- Board member, **Lewis Walpole Library**, Yale University

---

### The Higgs Boson Challenge (1993)

One of Waldegrave's most celebrated moments came in 1993 when, as British science minister, he offered a **prize for the best lay explanation of the Higgs boson**. Noting that British taxpayers were contributing heavily to CERN for something few understood, he challenged physicists to explain on a single page: "What is the Higgs Boson, and why do we want to find it?" Professor David Miller's winning entry — using a metaphor of Conservative party workers clustering around a former Prime Minister — became one of the most widely cited explanations of the Higgs mechanism.

---

### Private Sector

- Director of **Coutts & Company** (since 2012)
- Director of **Adam & Company** (2017–2018)
- Non-executive director of **GW Pharmaceuticals** (cannabis-based medicines)

---

### Personal Life

Waldegrave married **Caroline Burrows** in 1975. Caroline is a cookery writer and managing director of Leith's School of Food and Wine (and now Baroness Waldegrave of North Hill). They have four children: Katherine, Elizabeth, James, and Harriet.

He attended **Bilderberg Group** meetings four times: 1987, 1988, 1990, and 1995.

His memoir, **"A Different Kind of Weather"** (Constable, 2015), discusses his youthful political ambition, his career across the Heath, Thatcher, and Major governments, and his growing acceptance that he would not achieve his ultimate ambition of becoming party leader or prime minister.

---

### Coat of Arms Motto

*Coelum Non Animum* — "Always The Same Person"
